title: Liquidity picking and fund performance
abstract: Using global mutual fund and American Depositary Receipt (ADR) data, we test if funds strategically trade cross-listed firms’ equity shares in the most liquid trading location. We find that especially funds that score high on traditional skill measures exhibit a liquidity-based trading venue preference. We identify an informed trading motive as the most likely driver for such behaviour rather than preference based on geographic, economic, cultural, or governance motives. Thus, liquidity picking is associated with fund outperformance and stock selection ability that is not limited to only cross-listed firms. Our tests directly support theories of informed trading in a multi-market setting.

author.Feng Jiao: University of Lethbridge, Dhillon School of Business, Calgary, AB T2G 4V1, Canada

article.1. Introduction: The recent decades have witnessed two parallel processes related to the ongoing financial and economic globalization. The first one is the expansion of the mutual fund industry in many countries around the world. The second is the proliferation of stock cross-listings in many countries, especially in the United States.1 Motivated by these two developments, we raise the following questions. Given that investing in the same equity is possible in the firm’s home market or its chosen foreign listing location, do funds (skilled or unskilled) show any location preference for holding or trading the equity of a cross-listed firm? Given the near-perfect return correlation of cross-listed stocks with their home market counterparts (Gagnon and Karolyi, 2010), what are the reasons for such a location preference, if it exists?There are many potential benefits of cross-listings for firms, especially those placed in the United States.2 However, most of these benefits, including cost of capital reduction, visibility enhancement, equity offerings, foreign acquisitions, information generation, and disclosure stringency are firm-specific and not specific to the trading location: they may persuade investors to start investing in the equity of a cross-listed firm in general but have little relevance to investors’ location choices for trading a firm’s equity in its domestic market or on a US exchange. For instance, since the returns of cross-listed stocks are nearly identical across trading venues by the no-arbitrage principle, risk-based explanations cannot justify a specific location preference. Even on the matter of improved governance or investor protection when holding an ADR versus the firm’s equity in the home market, Licht et al. (2018) point out that in 2010 the US Supreme Court decided that the US securities laws would not apply to investment activities outside the United States, thus, effectively excluding US traded cross-listed firms from US antifraud enforcement.3 We note that, in spite of lower potential liabilities of US-listed foreign firms, their bonding preference for the US market may still prevail among some funds (Gagnon and Karolyi, 2018).Of the remaining cross-listing placement considerations, some could still conceivably induce a location preference for funds. Those include a preference based on proximity or familiarity differences between the fund and firm’s trading venues (e.g., geographic, economic, and cultural proximities, as in Sarkissian and Schill (2004)4 and a preference based on security-level liquidity differences between trading venues.In this paper, we focus on the aforesaid subset of cross-listing motivations and test the hypothesis that when investing in a cross-listed stock, funds display a location preference rooted in the desire to trade the firm’s equity in its most liquid trading venue. Such a preference should be particularly pronounced for skilled funds that seek to better exploit their informational advantages without significant effect on stock prices. This is the central hypothesis of our paper.Our hypothesis is motivated by theory and studies that examine the distribution of trading volume of cross-listed stocks. A central prediction common to theories of multi-market trading is liquidity concentration in one trading venue as especially informed (or “skilled”) investors strategically trade in the location where the market is the “thickest” to exploit their information with minimal price impact (Admati and Pfleiderer, 1988); (Chowdhry and Nanda, 1991).5 Also, empirical studies attest to the large heterogeneity and dynamics in the foreign share of trading volume of cross-listed stocks (Baruch et al., 2007); (Halling et al., 2008).We test our hypothesis using data on mutual funds with a global investment mandate from 35 countries (or territories) and firms from 40 countries (or territories) with their share listings as American Depositary Receipts (ADRs) on regular US exchanges.6 This is a uniquely powerful setting to test our main theoretical prediction and contrast it against alternatives that predict a location preference based on differences in various proximity metrics or investor protection. First, focussing on funds with global investment mandates rules out that any location preferences are implicitly driven by geographic mandate or style restrictions. Second, for those funds, cross-listed firms constitute over 20% of their average portfolio allocation, allowing us to measure and examine liquidity preferences for a meaningful part of funds’ overall investment decisions. Third, we show that funds do change the trading venue for cross-listed stocks over time. Fig. 1 shows the proportion of global funds per year that change the location of at least one stock in their portfolio between stock’s domestic trading venue and the US market. The proportion is quite sizable ranging from 31% in 2001 to about 12% in 2021. Fourth, a large literature on fund performance has identified a variety of proxies that allow us to identify “skilled” funds and contrast their investment behaviour when investing in cross-listed firms against the behaviour of “unskilled” funds. Fifth, our alternative motives for the selection of a specific trading venue (geographic, economic, and cultural proximities and investor protection) are already known to affect fund portfolio choice.Download: Download high-res image (166KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Proportion of funds with changing stock trading location per yearThis figure shows the proportion of global mutual funds each year that change the trading location for at least one stock in their portfolio between the stock’s domestic trading venue and the US where the ADR is listed. The sample contains funds from 35 countries and firms from 40 countries that cross-list in the United States.To measure the fund managers’ preference for security-level liquidity, i.e., trading of a given firm’s stock in its more liquid venue, we first construct a security liquidity picking (SLP) measure as the average fraction of the more liquid security (stock or ADR) over total equity holdings (stock and ADR) across all cross-listed stocks in the portfolio of the fund.7 SLP is computed over cross-listed stocks in the United States that are held in funds’ portfolios, since we can determine the trading locations of those stocks based on differences in security identifiers.8 The SLP measure is the key to our research question: We are interested in, not whether a fund invests in cross-listed stocks per se, but, conditional on investing, in where the fund trades the stock.9 We conjecture that funds that consistently select to trade in the most liquid venue exhibit strategic behaviour that theory associates with informed (or “skilled”) investing.Our empirical analysis is composed of several parts. We start with examining the determinants of a fund’s perfect SLP score to understand what type of funds exclusively trade in the most liquid trading venue. We sort funds based on various established skill metrics including active share (Cremers and Petajisto, 2009), the industry concentration index (“ICI”, Kacperczyk et al., 2005), and others and find that the fraction of funds with perfect SLP is the highest among the quintile of “most skilled” funds. For most measures, the representation of perfect SLP funds is monotonically increasing in the skill measure and two- to three-times higher in the top versus the bottom quintile. Multivariate tests that control for many fund characteristics confirm this observation: measures of skill are consistently positively correlated with liquidity picking, suggesting that more skilled funds are particularly careful in selecting their trading location. Empirically, our estimated effects indicate that for every 1.0% increase in the various measures of skill, the odds of a fund to exclusively trade in the most liquid trading venue increases by 2.3% to 4.3%. We find that this effect is similar for both US and non-US managed funds.We then test if the observed location preference of skilled funds is indeed reflecting a preference for trading in the most liquid trading venue rather than the most familiar trading venue or the one with better investor protection rules. We expand our sample and examine the location choice for each cross-listed portfolio position of each fund, relating the trading location choice for each portfolio position to the relative liquidity of the stock versus the ADR as well as their proximity and investor protection characteristics. Consistent with the past literature, we find that determinants like geographic or economic proximity affect observed portfolio holdings in the expected way: funds in general are more likely to hold the “closer” of the two securities. Furthermore, our estimates indicate that the effect of relative liquidity on location choice is, on average, comparable in magnitude to the effects of established proximity measures such as geographic or economic closeness.However, when conditioning on various “skill” measures, liquidity proves the most important determinant for skilled funds: the effect of liquidity on trading location choice is up to 50% stronger for skilled funds than for unskilled funds. We find no such consistent differential effect for skilled funds for any other motive that could induce a trading venue preference.Next, we examine the importance of liquidity picking for gross and risk-adjusted fund returns. For instance, based on univariate tests, we find that funds with perfect SLP (SLP = 1) outperform those with lower SLP (SLP < 1) by 85.2 bps per year in gross returns and between 40.8 bps and 82.8 bps per year in risk-adjusted returns depending on the performance evaluation model. Multivariate panel regressions with risk-adjusted returns and control variables support this evidence. Interestingly, the positive effect of SLP on fund performance survives controlling for established skill measures, indicating that liquidity picking is informative about fund manager skill beyond what is captured by existing proxies. A large battery of robustness tests based on multiple international factor models, US and non-US fund sub-samples, and net of fee fund returns confirm our finding. More importantly, we repeat our tests in the presence of alternative trading venue preferences (geographic, economic, and cultural proximities as well as investor protection). Even in the presence of these additional considerations, we find strong evidence of the relation between consistent security liquidity picking and fund performance. Among the alternatives, only geographic proximity shows some positive association with high returns among non-US funds.To sharpen identification, we conduct a series of event studies around instances when the more liquid trading location of a cross-listed stock switches from the home market to the United States or vice versa. While we identify 283 such instances attributable to 98 cross-listed firms, we find that for most of those cases, the distribution of liquidity across the two venues is far more homogenous than in the rest of the sample (i.e., while the ranking of the most liquid venue does switch, liquidity tends to remain fairly equally shared between both venues). Nevertheless, we still find that a small fraction of funds (2.0%) make an active decision to trade in the most liquid venue consistent with our hypothesis – their holdings of the cross-listed firms’ equity are partially or fully exchanged from the now less-liquid security to the now more liquid security trading in the alternative venue. This fraction is more than three times larger compared to the same statistic computed for other cross-listed stocks whose most liquid trading venue does not change. Consistent with the notion that liquidity chasing funds are skilled, we observe that funds that score above the median in standard skill metrics are more likely to engage in this practice than those with below median values. Then expectedly, we also find that funds that do follow stock liquidity switches register positive risk-adjusted outperformance between 59 bps to 123 bps the following year, supporting the conjecture that some funds make an active decision to trade in the most liquid venue and consistent with the central theoretical prediction that informed traders strategically select to trade a security in its most liquid venue to best capitalize on their information (Admati and Pfleiderer, 1988; Chowdhry and Nanda, 1991).In the final part, we conduct additional analysis using holdings-based fund returns. First, we show that liquidity picking is associated with stock selection ability (but not market timing ability) of fund managers and is not a sole effort to minimize transaction costs. Furthermore, perfect SLP funds also outperform in their holdings of non-cross-listed as well as foreign stocks, suggesting that such funds are “skilled” overall, not only specialized in trading cross-listed firms, and that our results are not driven by a preference for investing in geographically proximate firms (i.e., “home bias”). Second, we find a stronger positive relation between SLP and fund returns for large funds as well as for funds with a large share of small stocks in their portfolios. This result is consistent with the idea that selecting the most liquid trading venue is particularly useful for skilled funds that are more likely to suffer from capacity constraints in their trading strategies.We make two broad contributions to the literature. First, we link asset management research with the literature on cross-listed securities, taking advantage of the uniqueness of a global environment, where money managers around the world can select different markets for trading the same firm’s shares. This setting allows us to examine in detail the preferences of fund managers to invest in the firm’s equity in its domestic stock market or in the United States through ADRs.10 In doing so, we contribute empirically to the literature on informed trading in multi-market settings. While there are many papers in this field, they mainly focus on aggregate, market-level tests linking, for example, the market for a stock to trading in its derivatives and vice versa (e.g., Manaster and Rendleman, 1982; Easley et al., 1998; Chakravarty et al., 2004; Augustin et al., 2019). Few (if any) studies link strategic venue selection based on liquidity differences for the same security to investment performance at the investor or portfolio levels. Indeed, contrary to other related securities such as stocks and options that differ in multiple aspects including liquidity but also embedded leverage or the market environment in which they are traded, an ADR is practically identical to its underlying stock in all aspects but its liquidity. This greatly attenuates concerns about confounding factors that could lead to a location preference. The unique setting of global mutual funds selecting to trade the equity or the ADR of a cross-listed firm therefore allows us to directly test a key prediction of the theoretical literature: informed traders will strategically select the most liquid market for a security to best capitalize on their information.Second, we contribute to the literature that examines the investment behaviour of international mutual funds particularly that associated with informed trading. Such studies frequently identify specific investment biases and relate those to observed portfolio decisions and fund performance.11 Contrary to those studies, we are not interested in any portfolio biases or which funds over- or under-weight particular firms or markets. Instead, we focus on the choice of trading venue based on liquidity characteristics conditional on the fund investing in the equity of the cross-listed firm. That is, we do not seek to identify any possible source of information advantage but instead focus on testing a central prediction of theories of informed trading in a multi-market setting.
article.2. Hypothesis development: Our hypothesis is based on theories of informed trading in a multi-market setting (e.g., Admati and Pfleiderer, 1988; Chowdhry and Nanda, 1991; Baruch et al., 2007). The specific theories differ in the information structure that investors are endowed with, the location restrictions that investors face, or the drivers for emergence of a specific trading venue as the most liquid one. However, they all share one insight: informed investors in particular care about trading any given security in the most liquid market to exploit information advantages. In other words, informed investors are particularly attracted to trading in venues where the market is “thickest” because it allows them to capitalize on their information without generating large price impact.We highlight that we do not seek to differentiate between these specific theories but focus on testing their common key prediction. In doing so, we are agnostic as to which trading venue emerges as the more liquid one for any given cross-listed firm or why occasionally the most liquid venue switches. Instead, we exploit our unique setting as an opportunity to measure if funds trade in a manner consistent with the information advantage line of reasoning, and if so, what the characteristics of those funds are. This is our hypothesis:Hypothesis: Skilled funds trade in the most liquid venue to exploit information advantages.We translate this hypothesis into testable predictions adapted specifically to our setting. The key observation is that global mutual funds hold (and trade) both cross-listed and non-cross-listed stocks. This affords us an opportunity to measure liquidity picking for the subset of their cross-listed firm holdings. Furthermore, past studies identified various measures that allow for an ex-ante differentiation into “skilled” and “unskilled” funds, providing us a mapping to the “informed” versus “uninformed” investors that feature in the abovementioned theories. This allows for a direct translation of our hypothesis into our first empirical prediction:Prediction #1: Skilled funds trade in the most liquid venue for a cross-listed firm’s stock.Second, our hypothesis is cast in terms of a liquidity preference driving the venue choice of skilled funds. We recognize alternative drivers that can induce a venue choice and that can be correlated with a liquidity preference. We identify two such alternative motives – governance and proximity (familiarity) – and contrast our liquidity motive against them. On the topic of governance, it is generally believed that the United States provides the best minority investors’ protection (e.g., Doidge et al., 2004; 2007, 2009; Gagnon and Karolyi, 2018). If so, global funds, both within and outside of the United States, should always choose investing in ADRs rather than the actual stock in its domestic market, a possibility we discuss throughout our tests.Proximity considerations are supported by the extensive past cross-listing and international portfolio choice literatures. The proximity dimensions include geographic, economic, and cultural proximities and are frequently interpreted in the context of familiarity-based versus information-based investing.12 We provide explicit tests to differentiate our liquidity hypothesis from these alternatives. We note that the literature relates familiarity-based motives to a fund’s decision to invest (or not) in the firm’s equity in general leaving the choice of a trading venue aside. As highlighted in the introduction, our hypothesis focuses on the latter preference. Nevertheless, if, for example, geographic proximity affects investors’ portfolio choice, it is natural to expect that it could equally affect the funds’ trading venue selection due to their larger familiarity with the “closest” stock exchange. This is our second prediction:Prediction #2: Venue choice of skilled funds is not driven by proximity or governance motives.Third, the theories also agree that, in principle, all funds – skilled or not – would like to trade in the most liquid venue if only to minimize transaction costs. However, compared to “unskilled” funds (i.e., “noise traders”), “skilled” funds (i.e., “informed traders”) have an extra motive – exploiting their information advantage to minimize the price impact of their trades. Empirically, this means that the fund’s propensity to consistently trade in the most liquid market should reflect its strong performance in general based on various fund performance metrics, not only in the cross-listed portfolio of stocks. Moreover, it should be detected through those measures of fund performance that control for the simple motive of minimizing transaction costs such as holdings-based returns. This is our third prediction.Prediction #3: Skilled funds outperform beyond the holdings of cross-listed stocks.Having laid out our empirical predictions, we discuss several related and important points. To start, we iterate that, since returns on ADRs and their underlying stocks are practically the same by the no-arbitrage principle (Gagnon and Karolyi, 2010), the outperformance of funds under our hypothesis does not result from liquidity chasing per se. Instead, we argue that skilled funds care much more about trading in a more liquid venue, where they can exploit their informational advantage without large price impact concerns on their transactions.Empirically, we identify the most liquid trading venue for a cross-listed firm based on the relative trading volume of the ADR versus the stock on the firm’s home exchange. This choice is both consistent with the past empirical work on this subject (Baruch et al., 2007, Halling et al., 2008) and aligns directly with some other liquidity measures, particularly that of Amihud (2002). Indeed, since the returns of cross-listed firms’ stocks and their ADRs are practically identical, any difference in the Amihud (2002) liquidity between the stock and the ADR of a cross-listed firm is driven by the differences in their respective trading volumes.13Next, our tests focus on the fund’s choice to trade the stock of a cross-listed firm on its home exchange or through its ADR on a US exchange. Yet, investors can trade certain cross-listed firms in more than two venues: some firms have a tertiary listing of their stock on another exchange, while others also have a Global Depositary Receipt (GDR) program. However, since very few firms in our sample are affected by this and the vast majority of fund holdings is attributable to either the stock or the ADR, our main results are not impacted by the existence of alternative trading platforms for some of our cross-listed firms. For clarity, we maintain our general exposition as a choice of trading a firm’s stock on its domestic exchange or as an ADR.Finally, we acknowledge one element where our empirical strategy departs from the theory: our setting does not offer us a true equivalent of competitive market makers between exchanges. Indeed, there are other differences in market structure (e.g., different investor protection rules) that could induce a location choice. Our tests cannot differentiate between these possibilities but are instead designed around the established result that the most liquid trading venue differs across firms – the home exchange for some, the US for others – and, therefore, have power to identify (and reject) our main hypothesis that is based on liquidity differences.
article.3. Data and main variables: 3.1. Data sourcesThe dataset we employ in this study merges a sample of cross-listed firms with holdings data for international mutual funds. We describe both main data sources in turn. First, we construct a cross-listing firm sample from several sources and restrict it to placements on regular US exchanges (i.e., AMEX, NYSE, and NASDAQ) and from countries not classified as tax havens. We require that a cross-listed firm has both a domestic listing and an ADR, so that each fund always has a choice of trading a given firm in its domestic market or on a US exchange. We collect ADR data using the Citigroup ADR database and cross-check the data with foreign listing codes from CRSP and leave only those foreign firms that have identifiable SIC codes. We collect cross-listing information until 2019 because our fund holding and return data ends in December 2021 and we measure the trading venue choices of funds based on 1-year lagged holdings for cross-listed firms that have existed for that entire year. This procedure yields a final sample of 502 cross-listings (direct placements, Level II, and Level III ADRs) from 40 countries. Table A.1 in the Internet Appendix shows the distribution of cross-listed firms in the United States across countries over time and Table A.2 provides descriptive statistics of these firms.14Second, we obtain semi-annual portfolio holdings (June and December) for funds from the FactSet Ownership database. We complement this holdings data with additional fund information such as monthly fund performance and other fund characteristics from Morningstar Direct, section Global Open-End funds. Monthly return and AUM data are collected at the share class level for each fund and then aggregated to the fund level. From Morningstar, we also collect additional information including fees, family affiliation, inception and obsolete dates. We merge Morningstar and FactSet databases via links provided by FactSet and complement these links with a string-matching algorithm that compares fund names and that we verify manually as in Massa and Schumacher (2020). We identify holdings of cross-listed firms based on the sample described above. We distinguish between holdings of the firm’s domestic home listing and its ADR listing, since these two are technically different securities and have different security identifiers. This allows us to ultimately construct our main measure of liquidity picking that is described in the next sub-section.In our final sample, we focus on open-end, actively managed equity funds with total net assets (TNA) greater than 5 million USD and Morningstar-identified investment mandate for “Global Equity”. We identify this sample based on the Morningstar variable “globalcategory” that identifies the geographic investment focus for each fund and we select all funds that have a “global” mandate based on this classification. We deliberately include only funds with a global investment mandate in our sample, because we want to examine funds that face little to no geographic investment restrictions. We seek to minimize the chance that any location preferences are ultimately driven by geographic style or mandate restrictions. As a result, our sample of funds is more homogenous in terms of investment mandates compared to other studies that use international funds (e.g., Chuprinin et al., 2015; Schumacher, 2018; Massa and Schumacher, 2020).15Our overall sample spans from December 2000 to December 2021, and we require at least 24 monthly return observations per fund to compute the measures of fund performance. We complement the holdings and cross-listing data with international stock price and accounting data from DataStream and Worldscope.3.2. Variable definitionsOur main variable measures the fund’s location choice for security trading that we use throughout our tests. We call it security liquidity picking (SLP), which is the fund’s preference for holding the more liquid of the two securities, conditional on having any holding in a cross-listed firm. Specifically, SLP is defined as:(1)SLPi,t=1Si,t∑s=1Si,tMaxLiq(MVADRs,i,t,MVStocks,i,t)MVADRs,i,t+MVStocks,i,t,where i denotes a fund, t denotes a semi-annual period, and s denotes a cross-listed stock held in the portfolio of fund i in period t. MVADRs,i,t and MVStocks,i,t are the market values of fund i holdings of the ADR and the domestic listing of cross-listed stock s in period t, respectively. The numerator of Eq. (1) is the market value of the security that is more liquid for each cross-listed stock. As a baseline, we measure security specific liquidity of the stock and the ADR as the fraction of the overall trading volume that each of these securities has on average over the previous year. Given the near-perfect correlation of returns between ADRs and their domestic stock counterparts (Gagnon and Karolyi, 2010), we measure liquidity differences between those two securities by their respective share of the total trading volume for each firm as in Baruch et al. (2007) and others. In robustness tests, we measure liquidity differences between both securities based on bid-ask spreads. SLP is computed over all eligible cross-listed stocks held in the portfolio of fund i in period t. A fund that always (never) holds the more liquid of the two securities (ADR or its underlying stock) whenever investing in a cross-listed firm will have SLP equal to unity (zero).For the most part of our analysis, we focus on funds that consistently trade in the most liquid venue by defining an indicator variable equal to unity if SLPi,t=1 and zero otherwise. This choice is motivated by the fact that there is a large conceptual behavioural difference between perfect SLP and a “high” but non-perfect SLP funds. Indeed, unlike perfect SLP funds, funds even with relatively high but non-perfect of SLP do not consistently trade in the more liquid of the two markets (domestic or the United States) for a given firm security. Therefore, such funds do not seem to prioritize liquidity consistently across all decisions when exploiting possible informational advantages. In the context of our hypothesis, such consistent behaviour is what we expect from informed traders.In Fig. 2, we present a histogram of the average SLP over the time-series of each fund. As per Eq. (1), SLP is bounded between 0 and 1. Most funds score high on this measure: the median fund has an SLP of 0.87.16 At the same time, only a relatively small fraction of funds consistently trades the equity of a cross-listed firm in its most liquid venue for the entire sample period: about 7% or 292 funds have a “perfect” SLP = 1, meaning that at any point in our sample and across all their holdings of cross-listed stocks, these funds always hold the more liquid of the two securities. In our later tests, we measure a perfect SLP score as per the last disclosed portfolio of each fund. In those cases, the fraction of SLP = 1 funds is higher.Download: Download high-res image (146KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. Distribution of security liquidity picking measure (SLP)This figure shows the distributions of security liquidity picking (SLP) of global mutual funds from 35 countries and cross-listed firms in the United States from 40 countries. The sample period is 2001 to 2021 for each fund, it’s security liquidity measure is averaged over the entire sample period.Beyond our main measure for liquidity picking SLP, we also highlight a related control variable, which we call ADR investing (ADRI). ADRI is the fund’s unconditional preference for holding an ADR over the home market of listing of all cross-listed firms in the fund’s portfolio. It captures a fund’s preference for holding a US-listed security and, as mentioned in Section 2, there could be possible rationales motivating such a location preference, for example better investor protection and higher market-wide liquidity and efficiency. Specifically, ADRI is defined as:(2)ADRIi,t=1Si,t∑s=1Si,tMVADRs,i,tMVADRs,i,t+MVStocks,i,t,where the notation is as before but the numerator only considers the ADR holdings of the fund. In other words, ADRI measures the average fraction of each position in a cross-listed firm that the fund holds through the firm’s ADR. The average is computed over all cross-listed stocks held in the portfolio of fund i in period t. A fund that always (never) holds ADRs whenever investing in a cross-listed firm will have ADRI equal to unity (zero). As before, we define an indicator variable equal to unity if ADRIi,t=1 and zero otherwise for consistency with our main variable of interest SLPi,t=1.To test the alternative hypothesis for possible location preferences, we introduce several measures that capture differences in governance / investor protection and measures that capture proximity or familiarity based on geographic distance, economic distance (trade flows), or cultural distance (colonial ties and common language). These measures follow standard practice, and we define them when we introduce the specific tests. Likewise, all other control and outcome variables follow standard practice, and we present a full summary of variable definitions in the Appendix.3.3. article.4. Liquidity and venue choice: In this section, we test the first and second empirical predictions that skilled fund managers prefer to trade the equity of cross-listed firms in the most liquid trading venue rather than make their location choice based on proximity or governance considerations. We first conduct univariate and multivariate tests linking perfect SLP to various known skill measures of fund managers as well as to other fund characteristics. Then we horse-race security liquidity picking with other possible motives for specific trading venue selection of cross-listed stocks.4.1. Which funds trade in the most liquid venue?We start our investigation by performing simple univariate sorts based on several established proxies for investment skill. We consider active share (Cremers and Petajisto, 2009), industry concentration index (ICI, Kacperczyk et al., 2005), a tracking error based on R2 (Amihud and Goyenko, 2013), and the lagged four-factor fund alpha (Carhart, 1997).We present the results in Table 2. We report the percentage of funds that have perfect liquidity picking skills (SLP = 1) per quintile and the last two rows report the differences between the two groups and their corresponding z-score in the two-proportion z-test. We see, for example, that 34.2% of funds with the highest active share have perfect SLP and only 13.9% among low active share funds – almost 2.5 times as many. The difference in perfect SLP distribution between high and low quintiles is significant across all four skill proxies. Moreover, the share of SLP = 1 across quintiles is monotonically increasing for all measures except cumulative risk-adjusted return α(4F). Finally, the largest proportions of perfect SLP is observed for the highest quintiles of active share and ICI. Thus, this table provides first evidence that skilled funds particularly care about trading cross-listed firms in the most liquid venue.Table 2. Further, we find that SLP is positively related to a fund’s cash holdings – another dimension of liquidity management available to fund managers – that has, however, a direct performance cost (large cash holdings lower fund performance). SLP is also positively related to fund expenses and negatively related to turnover. Both columns also indicate that funds that only invests in ADRs of cross-listed firm (i.e., if ADRI = 1) have a weakly lower propensity to have a perfect SLP score. However, the result is of borderline significance at best.18Regression (3) and (4) add four established manager skill characteristics with the same set of fixed effects as in Regressions (1) and (2), respectively. The resulting estimation is consistent with the univariate tests from Table 2 and shows that high past skill proxies are positively associated with high SLP, i.e., skilled funds tend to pick a more liquid marketplace for stock trading. As in Table 2, this insight is consistently supported by all four skill measures and the economic magnitudes of the effects are comparable to those in Table 2. For example, for a 1.0% increase in Active_Share (evaluated at the mean), the odds ratio of a fund displaying a perfect SLP score increases by between 2.5% and 2.8%.19 For ICI or Tracking_Error, the same calculation suggests a 2.3% to 4.3% increase in the odds of displaying a perfect SLP score for every 1.0% increase in the skill measure (again, evaluated at the mean) .20 Table A.7, Panel A confirms these insights when identifying the most liquid venue based on bid-ask spreads instead of relative trading volumes.Interestingly, after controlling for these different skill proxies, we observe that ADRI = 1 becomes significantly negatively associated with SLP. This suggests that after controlling for skill, if funds have a propensity to consistently invest in the ADRs of cross-listed stocks, they tend to sacrifice liquidity in their trading which could indicate that there are indeed motivations to trade on US exchanges that are, however, separate from information-based motives for venue choice. The finding is also consistent with past studies that document large heterogeneity in the foreign share of trading volume in cross-listed stocks (e.g., Baruch et al., 2007) and findings in Halling et al. (2008), who show that for many cross-listed firms, liquidity remains highest in the domestic market.Finally, in Regressions (5) and (6), we split our sample into US and non-US funds and rerun Regression (4). Fund characteristics as expenses, turnover, or the ADRI = 1 indicator that had significant relation to perfect SLP in the overall sample retain their statistical importance only in one of the two sub-samples. More importantly, however, all four managerial skill measures, including past risk-adjusted performance, continue showing their positive impact on perfect SLP, and, with the exception of the tracking error in the US sub-sample, they all are significant as well. Among those active share and ICI are still significant at the 1% level in both US and non-US sub-samples of funds. Overall, these findings support our first empirical prediction.4.2. Do skilled funds value liquidity, proximity, or governance?We now turn to our second prediction and examine other factors that could induce a location preference for trading. These other candidate factors are our three proximity measures and the governance proxy. Our goal is two-fold: First, we want to establish that it is indeed liquidity characteristics that are important for the choice of trading venue as liquidity could conceivably be correlated with the alternative determinants. Second, we want to test if liquidity is particularly important for skilled funds when selecting their trading venue. Our first tests in Table 2, Table 3 indicate as much but do not have strong power to separate the liquidity motive from alternative ones. Our next test resolves this.To achieve our goal, we expand our dataset from the fund-period to the fund-position-period level. In other words, instead of measuring SLP at the aggregate fund level, we examine every position choice separately, asking if a funds’ propensity to trade the position in the cross-listed firm’s home stock market or the US exchange is related to liquidity characteristics or any of the above alternatives. We estimate the following fractional logistics model:(4)Homei,s,t=g0+g1Liquiditys,t−1+g2Liquiditys,t−1×Skilli,t−1+g3Alt_Motivesi,s,t−1+g4Alt_Motivesi,s,t−1×Skilli,t−1+gs+gi×t+εi,s,t,where the dependent variable, Homei,s,tis the proportion of holdings of fund i in a cross-listed firm s in period t in the domestic stock market of s. A value of 1 (0) means that fund i invests only in firm s’s home market (ADR) in period t. As explanatory variables we include Liquiditys,t−1, defined as an indicator equal to 1 if the home listing of cross-listed firm had higher trading volume than the firm’s ADR in period t-1 and 0 otherwise and a vector of alternative venue choice motives, Alt_Motivesi,s,t−1, that include geographic, economic, and cultural proximity as well as the investor protection considerations. All those proxies are defined as indicator variables equal to unity if the home market of the cross-listed firm scores higher on investor protection, or is closer in terms of geographic, economic, or cultural distance to the fund than the ADR and zero otherwise. Skilli,t−1 is one of the four measures of manager skill (also transformed in an indicator equal to 1 if the skill measure is above the sample median and 0 otherwise for easier interpretation of the coefficients).The main independent variable of interest is the interaction term between skill and liquidity that highlights if liquidity remains (or is even more) important for the investment behavior of skilled funds. A positive and significant is g2 implies that funds with above-median investing skill especially appreciate the benefit of trading a firm’s equity in the more liquid venue. Likewise, we include interaction terms for all other possible determinants to clearly understand the differences in venue choice for skilled versus unskilled funds along all dimensions. Model (4) is estimated by quasi-likelihood methods of Papke and Wooldridge (Papke and Wooldridge, 1996). All regressions include cross-listed firm fixed effects (denoted gs) to control for other fixed firm characteristics and fund times period effects (gi×t) to identify the drivers of venue choice for each fund portfolio. Standard errors are clustered by fund.Table 4 shows the estimation results. In Regression (1) we test the reduced form of Model (4) without interaction terms to understand if our various proxies are, on average, important in explaining observed trading venue choices, without conditioning on fund skill. We find that, out of five independent variables, liquidity, geographic and economic proximities are associated with a higher proportion of holdings of firm shares in the firm’s domestic market. The estimated effects are quantitatively important as well: If the fund location is geographically (economically) closer to the cross-listed firm’s home market, the proportion of the fund’s holding in the firm’s home market increases by 56% (49%). Interesting for our hypothesis, the same estimation indicates that the economic significance of relative liquidity (after controlling for all alternative determinants) is on par with the other effects: If the cross-listed firm’s equity shares are more liquid in the firm’s home market (relative to the United States), the proportion of the fund’s holding in the firm’s home market increases by 70%. These findings echo the large literature on proximity or familiarity-based investment with a novel addition that for cross-listed firms, security-level liquidity differences also matter for the average fund.Table 4. article.5. Security liquidity picking and fund p: In this section, we provide several tests for our third empirical prediction. That is, we show that the main motive behind skilled funds’ security liquidity picking is informed trading, which is detectable in general fund performance. We first analyse overall fund performance based on gross and risk-adjusted returns without and with alternative trading venue preferences. Then, we examine fund performance and managerial characteristics when funds switch their trading of a given firm between its domestic market and the United States in response to changes in the dominant trading volume and liquidity venue for its shares.5.1. Main performance resultsHaving established a positive relation between skilled funds and their subsequent preference for trading cross-listed stocks in their more liquid trading location (domestic versus the United States), we now move to examining a direct relation between perfect SLP and fund returns. We begin by looking at the relation between SLP to gross and various risk-adjusted fund returns in a univariate and then multivariate settings.We consider five measures of risk-adjusted returns (alphas) all of which benchmark excess fund gross returns (before fees) against different international factor models. For all alpha calculations, we first regress excess fund returns over (−37 to −1) months on the different factors to estimate factor loadings, and second compute the difference between excess fund returns in a given period and predicted excess fund returns using the estimated loadings. We require at least 24 fund return observations. The exact definition of each factor model is provided in the Appendix. We employ a large variety of risk adjustments, recognizing that a consensus on the appropriate factor model for international funds has yet to emerge in the literature. Our approach is consistent with past studies that examine risk-adjusted performance for international funds that also rely on a variety of factor corrections (e.g., Chuprinin et al., 2015; Cremers et al., 2016; Schumacher, 2018; Massa and Schumacher, 2020; Jagannathan et al., 2022).Table 5 presents the univariate test results. We split funds into two groups based on their SLP values, those with SLP < 1 (Low SLP) and those with perfect SLP = 1 (High SLP) and then examine average performance across both groups for the full sample of funds. The last row shows the difference in returns between the high and low SLP groups. The corresponding t-statistics for all point estimates are also shown. The test results are intriguing. The SLP High – Low difference in gross returns is positive 0.071 and highly significant. This implies that perfect SLP funds outperform their peers with SLP < 1 by 7.1 bps per month or 85.2 bps per year. The High – Low return difference does not drop substantially from the risk adjustment. The range of this difference is from 3.4 bps per month (40.8 bps per year) for the three-factor model to 6.9 bps per month (82.8 bps per year) for the eight-factor model. This result integrates well with our prior findings that attributes liquidity picking as an attribute of skilled funds.Table 5. article.6. Additional tests with holding period: Our finding that the ability of fund managers to select a more liquid venue for the same company’s stock is positively related to funds’ gross and risk-adjusted returns leaves the question on the mechanism of these funds’ outperformance open. Also, it is unclear whether this outperformance is solely (or largely) due to direct transaction cost minimization as highlighted in Section 2 rather than to trading on information advantages as postulated in our third empirical prediction. Therefore, in this section we provide additional evidence to substantiate our support for the third prediction using holdings-based fund returns.First, we examine various holding return measures to understand in what dimensions perfect SLP funds outperform. In particular, we determine whether such funds have superior stock selection and/or market timing ability and whether they also outperform in their holdings of non-cross-listed stocks. Second, we test which funds benefit more from liquidity picking by relating SLP to capacity constraints that certain funds may face.6.1. Liquidity picking and fund performance decompositionTo understand whether the security liquidity picking reflects stock selection ability of fund managers or simply reveals their efforts to minimize transaction costs without exploiting any firm-specific information, we use holdings-based returns. The test results in the previous section are based on the overall fund returns (before fees) but after transaction costs. In contrast, holdings-based returns do not incorporate transaction costs and therefore allow us to assess if the overall fund returns are higher because of superior stock selection (or market timing) ability or simply because high SLP funds are more efficient at lowering transaction costs.We repeat Model (5), now estimated at the semi-annual frequency to reflect the semi-annual holdings data and use as dependent variables seven different holdings-based return measures. The first measure, R(AH), is the semi-annual holding period return constructed from the holdings of all stocks held in the portfolio of a fund in a given period and the subsequent semi-annual stock return of each stock. The second and third measures, R(FH) and R(DH), decompose overall holding returns into foreign and domestic stock holding returns, respectively. We refer to holdings as domestic (foreign) if a fund managed in a given country holds a firm (its stock or ADR) domiciled in the same (different) country.25 The next two measures are R(CL) and R(Non-CL) that correspond to the holding returns of the cross-listed stock sample and non-cross-listed firms, respectively. The sixth measure, R(H-SVM), adjusts holding period returns by subtracting the return of a global size-value-momentum sorted benchmark portfolio across all stocks in Worldscope. Finally, the seventh measure, R(MT), is the holding-based measure of market timing relative to the same global measure as in Daniel et al. (1997).Table 10 shows the results of panel regressions of semi-annual holdings-based fund returns on SLP and the same set of control variables as in Table 6, column 3. Consistent with previous results for risk-adjusted fund returns, column 1 shows a positive and significant at the 1% level relation between perfect SLP and holdings return based on all stocks in the fund portfolio. The next two columns reveal that the full sample results are driven only by foreign holdings of global mutual funds. For this type of holdings, the coefficient on the SLP indicator is again positive and significant at the 1% level. Therefore, columns 2 and 3 substantiate again that our finding of liquidity picking among skilled fund managers is unlikely resulting from proximity-based investing.Table 10. R20.880.860.580.780.860.170.03Next, we examine holding returns from the sample of cross-listed and non-cross-listed stocks. Our SLP measure is constructed from the holdings of cross-listed stocks only, because it is only for those stocks that we can identify the location in which the position must have been established. Therefore, the outperformance of high SLP funds could be driven by their “specialization” in trading cross-listed stocks. However, under our hypothesis, liquidity picking is not a measure of specialization in cross-listed stock but a preference for skilled funds in general and therefore a reflection of a broader dimension of manager skill that is only empirically measurable from certain positions.We address this question head-on in columns 4 and 5 of Table 10, where we test the ability of funds managers with SLP = 1 to invest in outperforming cross-listed and non-cross-listed firms separately. We observe that the coefficient on SLP = 1 is positive and significant at the 1% level in both columns. These results rule out that perfect SLP funds merely “specialize” in picking or trading cross-listed stocks. Instead, security liquidity picking, even though it can only be measured from the holdings of cross-listed stocks, is a strategic behaviour displayed by informed investors in general, as predicted by theory. Finally, in columns 6 and 7, we follow Daniel et al. (1997) and examine whether perfect SLP funds have superior stock selection ability of their holding returns is adjusted for the characteristic-based global portfolio return and whether such funds possess any market timing ability, respectively. We find economically and statistically significant evidence of better stock selection ability among funds with SLP = 1, but, similar to many other studies, observe no indication that these funds have market timing ability.6.2. Liquidity picking and capacity constraintsFinally, we analyse whether liquidity picking of fund managers is particularly beneficial for funds that likely face capacity constraints. Indeed, the importance of proper liquidity management increases when portfolio managers handle less liquid securities. Moreover, additional liquidity issues could arise with trading by large funds, the actions of which may impact security prices, thus, lowering the liquidity of their transactions.Two dimensions that are likely to correlate with capacity constraints are funds trading in small-cap stocks that are generally less liquid and large funds that may be more concerned about their trades having a price impact. Therefore, to address the issue of interest, in each period, we divide the full sample of funds into four groups by fund TNA and average stock size held in the fund’s portfolio. We create two indicator variables that serve as capacity constraints proxies. The first variable, Small_Cap, is an indicator that equals one if the average lagged stock size is in the lowest quintile, and zero otherwise. The second variable, Large_Fund, is an indicator that equals one if the lagged fund TNA is in the largest quintile, and zero otherwise. We then re-estimate Model (5) and augment it with additional interaction terms between the SLP = 1 indicator and our two proxies for capacity constraints.Table 11 shows the tests results on how the two capacity constraint proxies affect the relation between perfect SLP and fund performance. As dependent variables, we use the holding returns on all stocks in Regressions (1) to (3) and the holding returns from cross-listed stocks only in Regressions (4) to (6). For holding returns on all stocks, we do not find evidence of a pronounced performance association for SLP = 1 funds that invest heavily in small-cap stocks (column 1), possibly reflecting that global equity funds are, due to their mandates, investing across firm size categories across many countries. However, in Regressions (2) and (3), we find that the effect of perfect SLP is especially strong for large funds, indicating that those funds benefit the most from trading in the most liquid venue. In economic terms, fund managers of large funds with perfect security liquidity picking add on average 33.9 bps more to their semi-annual total holdings returns compared to small funds (i.e., the performance effect of SLP is more than double for large funds compared to small funds).Table 11. R20.880.880.880.870.870.87Next, we focus on the returns from holding cross-listed stocks only. If security-level liquidity picking is important for funds investing in small-cap stocks, it is more likely to be detected in an environment where we can directly observe the venue choice, especially when the stocks are possibly relatively illiquid overall. Consistent with this reasoning, we find the effects much pronounced for holding returns from cross-listed stocks in Regressions (4) to (6). In Regression (4), the interaction with the Small_Cap indicator becomes significant and highly economically relevant at 93.2 bps for semi-annual holding returns. The joint regression specification in column 6 confirms this, indicating that as far as capacity constraints are concerned, the benefits of consistently trading in the most liquid venue are particularly beneficial for investments in cross-listed firms.26
article.7. Conclusion: In this study, using data on global mutual funds from 35 countries and firms with ADRs on US exchanges from 40 countries, we examine the preference of fund managers to trade the same firm’s stock in the United States versus its local market in the more liquid trading venue. This setting allows us to test a central prediction common to theories of informed trading in multiple markets (Admati and Pfleiderer, 1988; Chowdhry and Nanda, 1991) namely that, ceteris paribus, informed investors strategically choose to trade in the most liquid trading venue to best capitalize on their information. Using the setting of stocks and ADRs, we are able to link strategic venue selection based on liquidity differences for the same security to investment performance at the fund portfolio level.In our analysis, we construct and use a novel measure of managers’ trading venue preference – a firm-specific liquidity selection metric – security liquidity picking (SLP). We show that funds with perfect SLP, i.e., funds that always trade the stock of a given company in the more liquid market of the two possible are predominantly skilled funds based on previously established measures of skill and consistent with the theoretical prediction that such behaviour is of particular value to skilled investors. Consequently, we find that liquidity picking is associated with superior gross, net, risk-adjusted, and holdings-based fund returns as well as in both US and non-US fund sub-samples. Importantly, our results survive after controlling for standard fund characteristics, existing manager ability metrics, as well as funds’ alternative possible preferences for trading their firm’s security in a specific market such as geographic, economic, and cultural biases or better investor protection.We also examine fund liquidity chasing and performance around events when the more liquid trading location of a cross-listed stock switches from the home market to the United States or in the opposite direction. Skilled funds are more likely to correctly switch their holdings from the previously more liquid to the now more liquid venue and when doing so, register strong outperformance in the next year, indicating that these funds indeed acted to capitalize on superior information. Further tests reveal that funds with perfect SLP possess genuine stock selection skills rather than simply minimizing their transactions costs. We observe that such SLP is associated with superior fund returns not only for cross-listed firms but also for firms that are traded only in their domestic markets. Finally, consistent with the common understanding of more importance for proper liquidity management for potentially less liquid securities, we observe a stronger relation of perfect SLP to fund returns among funds with large proportion of small cap stocks in their portfolios and among large funds. Our study provides strong support to a group of theories of informed trading in a multi-market setting and our empirical measure that captures this behaviour incorporates information about skill that goes beyond existing skill measures.
